title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: Six paragraphs have been removed from the text. Choose the correct paragraph (A-G) to fill each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.title: Dust in the Void: The Archaeology of Space
content.text: Archaeology is traditionally defined by the study of the human past through material remains left in the earth. However, as our species has ventured beyond the atmosphere, the 'record' of our presence has expanded to the moon, Mars, and the deep reaches of the solar system. || (41) || [41] This extraterrestrial heritage is now becoming a subject of serious academic study [/41]. Experts argue that the sites of early space exploration, such as the Apollo 11 moon landing spot, are just as significant as the ruins of Pompeii or the Great Wall of China. They represent the first time a terrestrial species left its home planet. || (42) || [42] One of the most pressing issues is that of preservation [/42]. On Earth, sites are protected by national laws and environmental factors. In space, however, there is no wind or rain to erode the footprints left by astronauts, yet the sites are vulnerable to cosmic radiation and micrometeorite impacts. || (43) || [43] Beyond these natural threats, there is the human element [/43]. With the rise of private space tourism and lunar mining, there is a growing risk that these historic sites could be accidentally destroyed or looted for 'space souvenirs.' Unlike historical sites on Earth, there is currently no enforceable international treaty to protect them. || (44) || [44] To address this, organizations like 'For All Moonkind' are lobbying for legal recognition [/44]. They want the UN to declare these sites as 'World Heritage Sites' in a way that respects the 1967 Outer Space Treaty, which forbids any nation from claiming sovereignty over the lunar surface. || (45) || [45] However, space archaeology isn't just about preserving the big moments [/45]. It also involves tracking the thousands of pieces of 'space junk' orbiting our planet—abandoned satellites, spent rocket stages, and even lost tools. Each piece tells a story of technological evolution and failure. || (46) || [46] This orbital record is a mirror of our industrial history [/46]. By studying the distribution and degradation of this debris, archaeologists can learn about the history of the Cold War, the rise of the digital age, and the increasing commercialization of the void. As we look to the stars, we are finding that the past is right there with us.

paragraphs.A: [46] Many of these objects have been in orbit for over sixty years [/46]. They represent a unique archive of 20th-century materials science, showing how different metals and plastics react to the extreme vacuum and temperature fluctuations of space.
paragraphs.B: [41] This realization has given birth to a new sub-discipline: space archaeology [/41]. Its practitioners don't use shovels; instead, they use high-resolution satellite imagery and archival data to document human activity across the solar system.
paragraphs.C: [44] The legal challenge is that space law was written to prevent conflict between nations, not to preserve culture [/44]. Adapting these Cold War-era rules to the era of private enterprise is a complex task for international lawyers.
paragraphs.D: [42] These iconic locations remain frozen in time, capturing the exact moment humans first stepped onto another celestial body [/42]. The tracks made by Neil Armstrong's boots are still there, perfectly preserved in the lunar dust.
paragraphs.E: [45] In fact, the sheer volume of this debris is now a significant hazard for current missions [/45]. Mapping it is not only a historical endeavor but a practical necessity for the safety of future space travel.
paragraphs.F: [43] The lack of an atmosphere means that these human traces could theoretically last for millions of years [/43]. This permanence makes them a unique category of archaeological site, far more durable than anything found on our own planet.
paragraphs.G: Space archaeology also looks at how humans might live in the future. Designers are studying the layouts of the International Space Station to understand how to build better colonies on Mars.

questions.explanation: B introduce el término 'space archaeology' y explica sus métodos, lo cual encaja con el texto principal que habla del 'academic study'.
D menciona las 'iconic locations' y las huellas de Armstrong, conectando con el texto principal sobre los sitios de la exploración lunar.
F explica por qué las huellas lunares son tan duraderas (falta de atmósfera), siguiendo el punto sobre la preservación.
C detalla el 'legal challenge' y la era de la Guerra Fría, complementando la mención a 'lobbying for legal recognition'.
E conecta el 'space junk' (basura espacial) con el peligro para misiones actuales, expandiendo el tema más allá de los 'grandes momentos'.
A describe los objetos que llevan 60 años en órbita y su valor científico, cerrando el tema del 'orbital record'.
